content:
$$\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x=a \log |1+\sin x|-b \log |2+\sin x|+c$$ 
then the value of $a$ and $b$ is ----------------
explanation:
Let $$I=\int\frac{\sin2x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)}\,dx.$$

Substitute $t=\sin x$, so $dt=\cos x\,dx$ and $\sin2x=2\sin x\cos x=2t\cos x$.

Then

$$I=\int\frac{2t\cos x}{(t+1)(t+2)}\,dx =\int\frac{2t}{(t+1)(t+2)}\,dt.$$

Do partial-fractions:

$$\frac{2t}{(t+1)(t+2)}=\frac{A}{t+1}+\frac{B}{t+2},$$

so $2t=A(t+2)+B(t+1)=(A+B)t+(2A+B)$.

Matching coefficients gives

$ A+B=2,\quad 2A+B=0 \;\Longrightarrow\;A=-2,\;B=4. $

Thus

$$I=\int\Bigl(-\tfrac{2}{t+1}+\tfrac{4}{t+2}\Bigr)\,dt =-2\ln|t+1|+4\ln|t+2|+C.$$

Re-substitute $t=\sin x$:

$$I=-2\ln|1+\sin x|+4\ln|2+\sin x|+C.$$

Matching

$$I=a\ln|1+\sin x|-b\ln|2+\sin x|+C$$

we read off

$$a=-2,\qquad -\,b=4\;\Longrightarrow\;b=-4.$$

Answer: $a=-2,\;b=-4$ (Option C).

First note that

$$e^{x\ln6}\;e^x = 6^x\,e^x = (6e)^x\,. $$

Hence

$$\int\bigl(e^{x\ln6}e^x\bigr)\,dx = \int(6e)^x\,dx = \frac{(6e)^x}{\ln(6e)} + C\,. $$

Since $\ln(6e)=\ln6+1$, we get

$$\phi(x)=\frac{(6e)^x}{1+\ln6}\,. $$

Answer: Option D.

content: $\int \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{1+2 \sin x \cos x}} d x=\varphi(x)+C$ Then $\varphi(x)=$
explanation:
We start by noting the perfect‐square identity:

$$1 + 2\sin x\cos x \;=\;\sin^2x+\cos^2x+2\sin x\cos x \;=\;(\sin x+\cos x)^2\,. $$

Assuming $\sin x+\cos x>0$ so that

$$\sqrt{1+2\sin x\cos x} \;=\;|\sin x+\cos x|\;=\;\sin x+\cos x\,, $$

the integrand becomes

$$\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sin x+\cos x}\;=\;1\,. $$

Hence

$$\int\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{1+2\sin x\cos x}}\,dx =\int1\,dx =x + C\,. $$

So

$$\varphi(x)=x\,, $$

which is Option B.

First, set

$$u=\tan^{-1}x\quad\Longrightarrow\quad du=\frac{dx}{1+x^2},\quad x=\tan u\,. $$

Then

$ \int \frac{e^{\tan^{-1}x}}{1+x^2}\,(1+x+x^2)\,dx =\int e^u\,(1+\tan u+\tan^2u)\,du =\int e^u\bigl(\tan u+\sec^2u\bigr)\,du. $

Notice

$ \frac{d}{du}\bigl(e^u\tan u\bigr) =e^u\tan u+e^u\sec^2u =e^u\bigl(\tan u+\sec^2u\bigr), $

so the integral is

$ e^u\tan u+C =\;x\,e^{\tan^{-1}x}+C. $

Answer: Option B.

Let’s set up a partial‐fraction decomposition

$$\frac{x}{(x-1)(x-2)^2}=\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x-2}+\frac{C}{(x-2)^2}.$$

Then

$$x \;=\;A(x-2)^2+B(x-1)(x-2)+C(x-1).$$

Expanding and matching coefficients gives the system

• $A+B=0$

• $-4A-3B+C=1$

• $4A+2B-C=0$

Solving yields $A=1,\;B=-1,\;C=2$. Hence

$$\frac{x}{(x-1)(x-2)^2} =\frac1{x-1}-\frac1{x-2}+\frac2{(x-2)^2}$$

and integrating term by term:

$$\int\frac{x}{(x-1)(x-2)^2}\,dx =\ln|x-1|-\ln|x-2|-\,\frac{2}{x-2}+C =\ln\Bigl|\frac{x-1}{x-2}\Bigr|-\frac{2}{x-2}+C.$$

Comparing with

$$a\ln\Bigl|\frac{x-1}{x-2}\Bigr|+\frac{b}{x-2}+c$$

we read off

$$a=1,\quad b=-2.$$

Answer: Option C.

The easiest way is a secant‐substitution. Set

$$x=\frac{3}{2}\sec\theta,\quad dx=\frac{3}{2}\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta,\quad \sqrt{4x^2-9}=3\tan\theta.$$

Then

$$\int\frac{dx}{x\sqrt{4x^2-9}} =\int\frac{\frac{3}{2}\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta} {\bigl(\frac{3}{2}\sec\theta\bigr)\,(3\tan\theta)} =\frac13\int d\theta =\frac{\theta}{3}+C.$$

Since $\tan\theta=\dfrac{\sqrt{4x^2-9}}{3}$, we get

$$\int\frac{dx}{x\sqrt{4x^2-9}} =\frac{1}{3}\tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\sqrt{4x^2-9}}{3}\Bigr)+C.$$

That’s Option D.

Let’s complete the square in the denominator:

9 + 8x − x² = −(x² − 8x − 9) = −[(x − 4)² − 25] = 25 − (x − 4)².

So the integral becomes

$$\int\frac{dx}{\sqrt{25-(x-4)^2}}\,. $$

With the substitution $u=x-4$ and $a=5$, we use

$$\int\frac{du}{\sqrt{a^2-u^2}}=\sin^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{u}{a}\Bigr)+C.$$

Hence

$$\varphi(x)=\sin^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{x-4}{5}\Bigr),$$

which is Option B.

Let’s set

$$u=5-\frac x2,\quad du=-\frac12\,dx\ \Longrightarrow\ dx=-2\,du.$$

Then

$$\int \tan^2\!\bigl(5-\tfrac x2\bigr)\,dx =-2\int \tan^2u\,du =-2\int(\sec^2u-1)\,du =-2\bigl(\tan u-u\bigr)+C.$$

Substitute back $u=5-\tfrac x2$:

$$-2\tan\Bigl(5-\tfrac x2\Bigr)+2\Bigl(5-\tfrac x2\Bigr)+C =-2\tan\Bigl(5-\tfrac x2\Bigr)-x+\tilde C.$$

That matches Option D.

Rewrite the integrand:

$$\log x^2 = 2\ln|x|.$$

So

$$\int \log x^2\,dx = 2\int \ln|x|\,dx.$$

Use integration by parts on $\displaystyle\int \ln|x|\,dx$:

Let $u=\ln|x|$, $dv=dx$.

Then $du=\frac{1}{x}dx$, $v=x$.

So

$$\int \ln|x|\,dx = x\ln|x| - \int x\cdot\frac{1}{x}\,dx = x\ln|x| - x.$$

Multiply by 2:

$$2\bigl(x\ln|x| - x\bigr) = 2x\ln|x| - 2x.$$

Noting $2x\ln|x| = x\ln(x^2)$, the final answer is

$$\boxed{x\ln(x^2) - 2x + C},$$

which matches Option D.

Let

$ \frac1{(x+2)(x^2+1)} =\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{Bx+C}{x^2+1}. $

Clearing denominators gives

$ 1=A(x^2+1)+(Bx+C)(x+2) =(A+B)x^2+(2B+C)x+(A+2C). $

Equate coefficients:

$x^2$: $A+B=0$ ⇒ $B=-A$

$x^1$: $2B+C=0$ ⇒ $C=-2B=2A$

constant: $A+2C=1$ ⇒ $A+4A=5A=1$ ⇒ $A=\tfrac15$

So

$ B=-\tfrac15,\quad C=\tfrac{2}{5}, $

and

$ \frac1{(x+2)(x^2+1)} =\frac{1/5}{x+2}+\frac{-\tfrac15\,x+\tfrac{2}{5}}{x^2+1}. $

Integrate term by term:

$\displaystyle \int\frac{1/5}{x+2}\,dx=\frac15\ln|x+2|$,

$\displaystyle \int\frac{-\tfrac15\,x}{x^2+1}\,dx=-\tfrac{1}{10}\ln(x^2+1)$,

$\displaystyle \int\frac{2/5}{x^2+1}\,dx=\frac{2}{5}\tan^{-1}x$.

Hence

$ p=\tfrac15,\quad q=-\tfrac1{10},\quad r=\tfrac25, $

and

$ p+q+r=\frac15-\frac1{10}+\frac25=\frac{1}{2}. $

Answer: $\displaystyle \frac12$.

content: $$ \text { The value of } \int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{2 x-x^2}} \text { is } $$
explanation:
To evaluate the integral, we'll complete the square in the denominator and use a trigonometric substitution. Here's the breakdown:

1. Completing the Square:

The denominator is $$2x - x^2$$. To complete the square, we factor out a -1 and rearrange:

$$2x - x^2 = -(x^2 - 2x) = -(x^2 - 2x + 1 - 1) = -(x - 1)^2 + 1$$

2. Trigonometric Substitution:

Now, we use the substitution $$x - 1 = \sin \theta$$, which implies $$dx = \cos \theta \, d\theta$$. Also, $$(x - 1)^2 = \sin^2 \theta$$. Substituting into the integral, we get:

$$ \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{2x - x^2}} = \int \frac{\cos \theta \, d\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta}} $$

3. Simplifying and Integrating:

Using the identity $$1 - \sin^2 \theta = \cos^2 \theta$$, the integral simplifies to:

$$ \int \frac{\cos \theta \, d\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta}} = \int \frac{\cos \theta \, d\theta}{\cos \theta} = \int d\theta = \theta + C $$

4. Back Substitution:

Since we substituted $$x - 1 = \sin \theta$$, we have $$\theta = \sin^{-1}(x - 1)$$. Substituting back, we get:

$$ \theta + C = \sin^{-1}(x - 1) + C $$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: $$\sin^{-1}(x - 1) + C$$

content: $$ \int e^x\left[\frac{x^2+1}{(x+1)^2}\right] d x \quad \text { is equal to } $$
$$ \int \log x(\log x+2) d x \text { equals to } $$
explanation:
Let's solve the integral step by step:

We need to compute

$$ I = \int \log x(\log x+2)\,dx. $$

Step 1. Rewrite the integrand by distributing:

$$ I = \int (\log x)^2\,dx + 2\int \log x\,dx. $$

Step 2. Compute $ \int \log x \,dx $.

Use integration by parts:

Let $ u = \log x $ so that $ du = \frac{1}{x}\,dx $.

Let $ dv = dx $ so that $ v = x $.

Then,

$$ \int \log x\,dx = x\log x - \int x\cdot\frac{1}{x}\,dx = x\log x - \int 1\,dx = x\log x - x + C. $$

Step 3. Compute $ \int (\log x)^2\,dx $.

Again use integration by parts:

Let $ u = (\log x)^2 $ so that $ du = \frac{2\log x}{x}\,dx $.

Let $ dv = dx $ so that $ v = x $.

Then,

$$ \int (\log x)^2\,dx = x(\log x)^2 - \int x\cdot\frac{2\log x}{x}\,dx = x(\log x)^2 - 2\int \log x\,dx. $$

Substitute the result from Step 2:

$$ \int (\log x)^2\,dx = x(\log x)^2 - 2\left[x\log x - x\right] = x(\log x)^2 - 2x\log x + 2x. $$

Step 4. Combine the results:

$$ I = \Big[x(\log x)^2 - 2x\log x + 2x\Big] + 2\Big[x\log x - x\Big]. $$

Simplify the expression:

The terms with $ x\log x $:

$$ -2x\log x + 2x\log x = 0. $$

The terms with $ x $:

$$ 2x - 2x = 0. $$

Thus, we are left with:

$$ I = x(\log x)^2 + C. $$

Comparing with the given options, the correct answer is:

Option D:

$$ x(\log x)^2+C. $$

content: If $$\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sin ^3 x \cos x}} d x=\frac{k}{\sqrt{\tan x}}+c$$ then the value of $$k$$ is

explanation:
The given integral is:

$$ \int \frac{x}{x^4-16} d x $$

We can factor the denominator as a difference of squares:

$$ x^4-16 = (x^2-4)(x^2+4) = (x-2)(x+2)(x^2+4) $$

Now, we can use partial fraction decomposition to rewrite the integrand:

$$ \frac{x}{(x-2)(x+2)(x^2+4)} = \frac{A}{x-2} + \frac{B}{x+2} + \frac{Cx+D}{x^2+4} $$

To find the constants A, B, C, and D, we multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$$ x = A(x+2)(x^2+4) + B(x-2)(x^2+4) + (Cx+D)(x-2)(x+2) $$

We can solve for the constants by plugging in specific values of x and comparing coefficients.

For example, setting x = 2, we get:

$$ 2 = A(4)(8) \Rightarrow A = \frac{1}{16} $$

Similarly, setting x = -2, we get:

$$ -2 = B(-4)(8) \Rightarrow B = \frac{1}{16} $$

Setting x = 0, we get:

$$ 0 = 8A - 8B - 4D \Rightarrow D = 0 $$

Finally, comparing the coefficient of x^3 on both sides, we get:

$$ 0 = A + B + C \Rightarrow C = -\frac{1}{8} $$

Therefore, the integral becomes:

$$ \int \frac{x}{(x-2)(x+2)(x^2+4)} dx = \int \frac{1/16}{x-2} dx + \int \frac{1/16}{x+2} dx + \int \frac{-x/8}{x^2+4} dx $$

Now we can integrate each term separately:

$$ \int \frac{1/16}{x-2} dx = \frac{1}{16} \ln|x-2| + C_1 $$

$$ \int \frac{1/16}{x+2} dx = \frac{1}{16} \ln|x+2| + C_2 $$

$$ \int \frac{-x/8}{x^2+4} dx = -\frac{1}{16} \ln(x^2+4) + C_3 $$

Combining all the terms, we get:

$$ \int \frac{x}{x^4-16} dx = \frac{1}{16} \ln|x-2| + \frac{1}{16} \ln|x+2| -\frac{1}{16} \ln(x^2+4) + C $$

Using the properties of logarithms, we can simplify the result:

$$ \int \frac{x}{x^4-16} dx = \frac{1}{16} \ln \left| \frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{x^2+4} \right| + C $$

Simplifying further:

$$ \int \frac{x}{x^4-16} dx = \frac{1}{16} \ln \left| \frac{x^2-4}{x^2+4} \right| + C $$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C:

$$ \frac{1}{16} \log \left|\frac{x^2-4}{x^2+4}\right|+C $$

content: $$ \text { The value of } \int \frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x-1}} d x \text { is } $$

explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{x d x}{2(1+x)^{3 / 2}}$$

On putting, $$1+x=t$$, we get $$d x=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad I =\int \frac{(t-1) d t}{2 t^{3 / 2}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\int t^{-1 / 2} d t-\int t^{-3 / 2} d t\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{t^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}-\frac{t^{-1 / 2}}{-1 / 2}\right]+C \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2\left[\sqrt{t}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}\right]+C \\ & =\frac{t+1}{\sqrt{t}}+C=\frac{x+2}{\sqrt{1+x}}+C \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let} \quad I & =\int \frac{4^x}{\sqrt{1-16^x}} d x \\ & =\int \frac{4^x}{\sqrt{1-\left(4^x\right)^2}} d x \end{aligned}$$

On putting, $$4^x=t$$, we get $$4^x \log 4 d x=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad 4^x d x & =\frac{d t}{\log 4} \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{\log 4} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 4} \sin ^{-1} t+C \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 4} \sin ^{-1} 4^x+C \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int x^x(1+\log x) d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
To solve the integral $$\int x^x(1+\log x) \, dx,$$ we begin by recognizing that direct integration strategies such as substitution or integration by parts do not straightforwardly apply. Instead, we'll look for a pattern or simplification.

First, consider the term $x^x$. One way to differentiate $x^x$ is using the exponential and logarithmic transformation. Recall that:

$$ x^x = e^{\log(x^x)} = e^{x\log x}. $$

Using the chain rule and product rule, we differentiate:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}x^x = \frac{d}{dx}(e^{x\log x}) = e^{x\log x} \left(x \frac{1}{x} + \log x \right) = e^{x\log x} (1 + \log x) = x^x(1 + \log x). $$

This computation shows that the derivative of $x^x$ is indeed $x^x(1 + \log x)$:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}x^x = x^x(1 + \log x). $$

Now, let's integrate both sides:

$$ \int \frac{d}{dx}x^x \, dx = \int x^x(1 + \log x) \, dx. $$

This simplifies to:

$$ x^x = \int x^x(1 + \log x) \, dx. $$

Therefore, the integral $$\int x^x(1+\log x) \, dx$$ evaluates to $$x^x + C,$$ where $C$ is the constant of integration.

Comparing this to the options given, Option B is the correct answer:

$x^x + c$

content: $$ \int e^x\left(1+\tan x+\tan ^2 x\right) d x \text { is equal to } $$
explanation:
The integral given is:

$$ \int e^x\left(1+\tan x+\tan ^2 x\right) d x $$

We can simplify the expression within the integral using a trigonometric identity. Recall the identity:

$$ \sec^2 x = 1 + \tan^2 x $$

Using this identity, we can rewrite the integral as:

$$ \int e^x(\sec^2 x + \tan x) \, dx $$

To solve this integral, we will use the method of substitution. Let’s first focus on the part:

$$ \int e^x \tan x \, dx $$

We utilize integration by parts with the following choices:

  • Let $$ u = \tan x $$, then $$ du = \sec^2 x \, dx $$.
  • Let $$ dv = e^x \, dx $$, then $$ v = e^x $$.

Now by integration by parts formula, $$ \int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du $$, we get:

$$ \int e^x \tan x \, dx = e^x \tan x - \int e^x \sec^2 x \, dx $$

We can reorganize the integral equation to:

$$ \int e^x(\sec^2 x + \tan x) \, dx = \int e^x \sec^2 x \, dx + \int e^x \tan x \, dx $$

$$ = \int e^x \sec^2 x \, dx + e^x \tan x - \int e^x \sec^2 x \, dx $$

Here the term $$ \int e^x \sec^2 x \, dx $$ cancels out, leaving:

$$ \int e^x(\sec^2 x + \tan x) \, dx = e^x \tan x + C $$

So, the integral evaluated is:

$$ e^x \tan x + C $$

Option C is the correct answer:

$$ e^x \tan x + c $$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int \frac{1}{x \sqrt{a x-x^2}} d x \\ & \text { Putting } x=\frac{a}{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad d x=\frac{-a}{t^2} d t \\ & \therefore \quad I=\int \frac{1}{\frac{a}{t} \sqrt{a \cdot \frac{a}{t}-\frac{a^2}{t^2}}} \times-\frac{a}{t^2} d t\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{-t}{t^2 \sqrt{\frac{a^2}{t}-\frac{a^2}{t^2}}} d t \\ & =\int \frac{-1}{a t \sqrt{\frac{1}{t}-\frac{1}{t^2}}} d t \\ & =\frac{-1}{a} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{t^2}{t}-\frac{t^2}{t^2}}} d t \\ & =\frac{-1}{a} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{t-1}} d t=\frac{-1}{a} \int(t-1)^{-1 / 2} d t \\ & =\frac{-1}{a} \frac{(t-1)^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}+C=\frac{-2}{a}\left(\frac{a}{x}-1\right)^{1 / 2}+C \\ & I=\frac{-2}{a} \sqrt{\frac{a-x}{x}}+C \\ \end{aligned}$$

Option (c) is correct.

We have,

$$I=\int \frac{3^x}{\sqrt{1-9^x}} d x=\int \frac{3^x}{\sqrt{1-\left(3^x\right)^2}} d x$$

Putting $$3^x=t$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 3^x \log 3 d x=d t \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3^x d x=\frac{1}{\log 3} d t \\ & \therefore \quad I=\frac{1}{\log 3} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 3} \sin ^{-1} t+C \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 3} \sin ^{-1}\left(3^x\right)+C \\ \end{aligned}$$

Option (c) is correct.

$$ \text { Let } I=\int \frac{2^x}{\sqrt{1-4^x}} d x=\int \frac{2^x}{\sqrt{1-\left(2^x\right)^2}} d x$$

Putting $$2^x=t \Rightarrow 2^x \cdot \log 2 d x=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 2^x d x=\frac{d t}{\log 2} \\ & \therefore \quad I=\frac{1}{\log 2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{1-t^2}}=\frac{1}{\log 2} \sin ^{-1}(t)+C \\ & =\frac{1}{\log 2} \sin ^{-1}\left(2^x\right)+C \\ & \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Let } I=\int \frac{d x}{x^2\left[1+x^4\right]^{\frac{3}{4}}}=\int \frac{d x}{x^2\left(x^4\left(\frac{1}{x^4}+1\right)\right)^{3 / 4}} $$

[taking $$x^4$$ common from denominator]

$$\Rightarrow \int \frac{d x}{x^5\left(\frac{1}{x^4}+1\right)^{3 / 4}}$$

Let $$\left(\frac{1}{x^4}+1\right)=t^4 \quad \Rightarrow \frac{-4 d x}{x^5}=4 t^3 d t \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{x^5}=-t^3 d t$$

$$\therefore I=\int \frac{-t^3 d t}{t^3}=-t+C=-\left(\frac{1}{x^4}+1\right)^{1 / 4}+C$$

We have,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3 x^2+1}{x^2-6 x+8} & =3+\frac{18 x-23}{x^2-6 x+8} \\ & =3+\frac{18 x-23}{(x-4)(x-2)} \end{aligned}$$

Again, let $$\frac{18 x-23}{(x-4)(x-2)}=\frac{A}{x-4}+\frac{B}{x-2}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad 18 x-23=A(x-2)+B(x-4)$$

Put $$x=4$$, we get

$$72-23=2 A \Rightarrow A=\frac{49}{2}$$

Put $$x=2$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned}36-23 & =-2 B \\ \Rightarrow \quad B & =\frac{-13}{2} \\ \text { So, } \frac{3 x^2+1}{x^2-6 x+8} & =3+\frac{49}{2(x-4)}-\frac{13}{2(x-2)}\end{aligned}$$

Let

$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int \frac{1}{1+\cos 8 x} d x \\ & =\int \frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 4 x} d x=\frac{1}{2} \int \sec ^2 4 x d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\tan 4 x}{4}\right)+C=\frac{1}{8} \tan 4 x+C\end{aligned}$$

Let

$$I =\int \frac{x^2+1}{x^2-1} d x=\int \frac{x^2-1+2}{x^2-1} d x$$

$$=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x^2-1}\right) d x=x+2 \times \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right|+C$$

$$=x+\log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right|+C$$
